section.title: Jeremiah Imprisoned
section.sectionRationale: Zedekiah is a weak king caught between fearing Babylon and fearing his own officials, and he privately wants Jeremiah's word even while publicly letting his officials abuse the prophet. Jeremiah is arrested as a deserter, beaten, and lowered into a muddy cistern to die, then rescued by an unlikely foreigner. Through it all he keeps repeating the one message the king cannot bring himself to obey: surrender to Babylon and live.
section.thesis: A king who wants a comforting word but will not obey the true one leaves his faithful prophet beaten, imprisoned, and sinking in the mud, yet God preserves Jeremiah through an outsider's courage and keeps offering Zedekiah life if only he will surrender, right up to the last possible moment.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: Zedekiah's fragile reign and Jeremiah's warning during a brief lull in the siege; Jeremiah arrested at the city gate and thrown into prison as a deserter; Jeremiah lowered into a cistern to die and rescued by Ebed-melech the Cushite; and a secret royal audience in which Zedekiah hears the truth yet cannot bring himself to act on it.

units.label: Zedekiah's fragile reign and Jeremiah's warning
units.note: a false lull in the siege

verses.text: Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon made Zedekiah son of Josiah the king of Judah, and he reigned in place of Coniah son of Jehoiakim.
But he and his officers and the people of the land refused to obey the words that the LORD had spoken through Jeremiah the prophet.
Yet King Zedekiah sent Jehucal son of Shelemiah and Zephaniah the priest, the son of Maaseiah, to Jeremiah the prophet with the message, “Please pray to the LORD our God for us!”
Now Jeremiah was free to come and go among the people, for they had not yet put him in prison.
Pharaoh's army had left Egypt, and when the Chaldeans who were besieging Jerusalem heard the report, they withdrew from Jerusalem.
Then the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah the prophet:

“This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says that you are to tell the king of Judah, who sent you to Me: Behold, Pharaoh's army, which has marched out to help you, will go back to its own land of Egypt.
Then the Chaldeans will return and fight against this city. They will capture it and burn it down.
This is what the LORD says: Do not deceive yourselves by saying, ‘The Chaldeans will go away for good,’ for they will not!
Indeed, if you were to strike down the entire army of the Chaldeans that is fighting against you, and only wounded men remained in their tents, they would still get up and burn this city down.”

units.label: Jeremiah arrested as a deserter
units.note: beaten and jailed on a false charge

verses.text: When the Chaldean army withdrew from Jerusalem for fear of Pharaoh's army,
Jeremiah started to leave Jerusalem to go to the land of Benjamin to claim his portion there among the people.
But when he reached the Gate of Benjamin, the captain of the guard, whose name was Irijah son of Shelemiah, the son of Hananiah, seized him and said, “You are deserting to the Chaldeans!”

“That is a lie,” Jeremiah replied. “I am not deserting to the Chaldeans!” 
But Irijah would not listen to him; instead, he arrested Jeremiah and took him to the officials.
The officials were angry with Jeremiah, and they beat him and placed him in jail in the house of Jonathan the scribe, for it had been made into a prison.
So Jeremiah went into a cell in the dungeon and remained there a long time.

Later, King Zedekiah sent for Jeremiah and received him in his palace, where he asked him privately, “Is there a word from the LORD?” 
“There is,” Jeremiah replied. “You will be delivered into the hand of the king of Babylon.”
Then Jeremiah asked King Zedekiah, “How have I sinned against you or your servants or these people, that you have put me in prison?
Where are your prophets who prophesied to you, claiming, ‘The king of Babylon will not come against you or this land’?
But now please listen, O my lord the king. May my petition come before you. Do not send me back to the house of Jonathan the scribe, or I will die there.”

So King Zedekiah gave orders for Jeremiah to be placed in the courtyard of the guard and given a loaf of bread daily from the street of the bakers, until all the bread in the city was gone. So Jeremiah remained in the courtyard of the guard.

units.label: Thrown into the cistern, rescued by Ebed-melech
units.note: sunk in the mud, lifted out by a foreigner

verses.text: Now Shephatiah son of Mattan, Gedaliah son of Pashhur, Jucal son of Shelemiah, and Pashhur son of Malchijah heard that Jeremiah had been telling all the people:

“This is what the LORD says: Whoever stays in this city will die by sword and famine and plague, but whoever surrenders to the Chaldeans will live; he will retain his life like a spoil of war, and he will live.
This is what the LORD says: This city will surely be delivered into the hands of the army of the king of Babylon, and he will capture it.”

Then the officials said to the king, “This man ought to die, for he is discouraging the warriors who remain in this city, as well as all the people, by speaking such words to them; this man is not seeking the well-being of these people, but their ruin.”
“Here he is,” replied King Zedekiah. “He is in your hands, since the king can do nothing to stop you.”

So they took Jeremiah and dropped him into the {{bor|cistern}} of Malchiah, the king's son, which was in the courtyard of the guard. They lowered Jeremiah with ropes into the cistern, which had no water but only mud, and Jeremiah sank down into the mud.
Now {{ebedmelech|Ebed-melech}} the Cushite, a court official in the royal palace, heard that Jeremiah had been put into the cistern. While the king was sitting at the Gate of Benjamin,
Ebed-melech went out from the king's palace and said to the king,

“My lord the king, these men have acted wickedly in all that they have done to Jeremiah the prophet. They have dropped him into the cistern, where he will starve to death, for there is no more bread in the city.”
So the king commanded Ebed-melech the Cushite, “Take thirty men from here with you and pull Jeremiah the prophet out of the cistern before he dies.”

Then Ebed-melech took the men with him and went to the king's palace, to a place below the storehouse. From there he took old rags and worn-out clothes and lowered them with ropes to Jeremiah in the cistern.
Ebed-melech the Cushite cried out to Jeremiah, “Put these worn-out rags and clothes under your arms to pad the ropes.” Jeremiah did so,
and they pulled him up with the ropes and lifted him out of the cistern. And Jeremiah remained in the courtyard of the guard.

units.label: A secret meeting, and Jeremiah's silence kept
units.note: the truth offered again, and refused

verses.text: Then King Zedekiah sent for Jeremiah the prophet and received him at the third entrance to the house of the LORD. “I am going to ask you something,” said the king to Jeremiah. “Do not hide anything from me.”
“If I tell you,” Jeremiah replied, “you will surely put me to death. And even if I give you advice, you will not listen to me.”

But King Zedekiah swore secretly to Jeremiah, “As surely as the LORD lives, who has given us this life, I will not kill you, nor will I deliver you into the hands of these men who are seeking your life.”

Then Jeremiah said to Zedekiah, “This is what the LORD God of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: ‘If you indeed surrender to the officers of the king of Babylon, then you will live, this city will not be burned down, and you and your household will survive.

But if you do not surrender to the officers of the king of Babylon, then this city will be delivered into the hands of the Chaldeans. They will burn it down, and you yourself will not escape their grasp.’”
But King Zedekiah said to Jeremiah, “I am afraid of the Jews who have deserted to the Chaldeans, for the Chaldeans may deliver me into their hands to abuse me.”
“They will not hand you over,” Jeremiah replied. “Obey the voice of the LORD in what I am telling you, that it may go well with you and you may live.
But if you refuse to surrender, this is the word that the LORD has shown me:

All the women who remain in the palace of the king of Judah will be brought out to the officials of the king of Babylon, and those women will say: 
‘They misled you and overcame you, those trusted friends of yours. Your feet sank into the mire, and they deserted you.’
All your wives and children will be brought out to the Chaldeans. And you yourself will not escape their grasp, for you will be seized by the king of Babylon, and this city will be burned down.”
Then Zedekiah warned Jeremiah, “Do not let anyone know about this conversation, or you will die.
If the officials hear that I have spoken with you, and they come and demand of you, ‘Tell us what you said to the king and what he said to you; do not hide it from us, or we will kill you,’
then tell them, ‘I was presenting to the king my petition that he not return me to the house of Jonathan to die there.’”

When all the officials came to Jeremiah and questioned him, he relayed to them the exact words the king had commanded him to say. So they said no more to him, for no one had overheard the conversation.
And Jeremiah remained in the courtyard of the guard until the day Jerusalem was captured.

bor.gloss: A pit or cistern, ordinarily dug to store rainwater. This one held only mud, and the officials intend it as a slow, deniable execution, since Jeremiah would starve or suffocate rather than be visibly killed.

ebedmelech.gloss: Literally servant of the king, likely a title or throne name rather than a birth name. A foreign court official, a Cushite, bears this name and becomes the one man in the palace who acts to save God's true prophet from the men who serve the king in name only.

basic.context: Zedekiah is Babylon's puppet king, caught between officials who want to fight and a prophet who keeps saying surrender is the only way to live. When Egypt's army briefly draws the Chaldeans away from the siege, Jeremiah tries to leave the city on personal business and is arrested as a deserter, beaten, and jailed. Later, when he keeps telling the people to surrender, the officials lower him into a muddy cistern to die quietly, and only the courage of Ebed-melech, a foreign court official, saves his life. Zedekiah keeps summoning Jeremiah secretly for the truth, and keeps being too afraid of his own officials to act on it.
basic.hermeneutics: Read as history, this narrative shows exactly how a nation destroys its own last chance: not through ignorance, since Zedekiah hears the true word repeatedly and privately believes enough of it to protect Jeremiah's life, but through fear of other people's opinion overriding fear of God. The rescue by Ebed-melech, an outsider with no political stake in the outcome, is a deliberate contrast to the insiders who either persecute the prophet or, like the king, know the truth and still will not act on it.
basic.originalLanguages: Jeremiah is thrown into a bor, a cistern meant for water but here holding only mud, a slow and deniable death sentence. His rescuer bears the name Ebed-melech, servant of the king, and it is this servant, not the king himself, who acts with the courage the name implies, exposing how far the actual court had drifted from true service to either God or king.
basic.typology: The righteous prophet sunk in the mire of a pit, rescued only by an outsider's intervention, echoes Joseph thrown into a pit by his brothers and points forward to Christ, the greater righteous sufferer cast down by His own people and vindicated by God.
basic.christ: Jeremiah's descent into mud, left to die quietly rather than executed openly, and his rescue by an unlikely foreigner, prefigure the pattern fulfilled in Christ: the true word of God, hated by the very people it was sent to save, brought low, yet preserved and vindicated by God's own hand, with grace reaching even those outside the covenant people, as it reached Ebed-melech.
basic.god: God preserves His prophet's life through the most unlikely instrument in the palace, showing His providence is not limited to Israel's own officials or even its king. At the same time, God continues offering Zedekiah a genuine way to survive, right up to the last private audience, showing His patience keeps extending even to a king who has already shown he lacks the courage to obey.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 37:24 has Joseph's brothers throw him into an empty pit before selling him. Psalm 40:2 pictures being lifted out of a miry pit and set on a rock, the very rescue Jeremiah experiences. Acts 16 shows God preserving His servants through prison in a way that still accomplishes His purposes. Hebrews 11:36-38 lists prophets who suffered chains and imprisonment for their faithfulness.
basic.oneStory: A faithful prophet is beaten and left to die in the mud for telling the truth, yet God preserves him through an outsider's courage while a fearful king forfeits his last chance at life. This is the recurring shape of the whole Bible: the true word of God suffers at the hands of those it came to save, yet is never finally destroyed, a pattern completed in Christ, the greater prophet rejected, buried, and raised.

context.literary: A tightly interwoven double narrative, Jeremiah's arrest and imprisonment in chapter 37 and his near execution by cistern and secret royal audiences in chapter 38, both structured around the same repeated offer, surrender to Babylon and live, refused each time.
context.historical: Set during the final siege of Jerusalem under Zedekiah, roughly 588 to 587 BC, with a brief interruption when an Egyptian relief force under Pharaoh Hophra drew the Babylonian army away before it returned to finish the siege.
context.canonical: One of Scripture's clearest portraits of a genuinely fearful, double minded ruler, unlike the outright hostility of Jehoiakim, showing that knowing and privately believing God's word is not the same as having the courage to obey it.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Zedekiah's downfall came not from ignorance of God's word, since he sought it out repeatedly and in secret, but from fear of his own officials overriding his private convictions, while honoring Jeremiah's costly faithfulness and Ebed-melech's unlikely courage.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is historical narrative describing a particular king's failure of nerve during a particular siege; it does not prescribe civil disobedience or secrecy as a pattern for believers. What it does establish permanently is that private belief in God's word is worthless without the courage to obey it publicly.

debates.question: Was Zedekiah a believer who simply lacked courage, or was his private interest in Jeremiah's words merely superstitious hedging?

views.label: A believer paralyzed by fear
views.case: Zedekiah protects Jeremiah's life more than once, seeks him out secretly and repeatedly, and swears an oath in the LORD's name, suggesting some genuine regard for the word even though fear of his officials keeps him from acting on it.

views.label: A man hedging his bets without real conviction
views.case: Zedekiah never once obeys the word he receives, continues to let his officials abuse Jeremiah, and asks Jeremiah to lie to protect him, patterns more consistent with using the prophet for information than genuinely submitting to God.

debates.bearing: Either reading warns that knowledge of God's word, even sought out sincerely and repeatedly, saves no one without obedience that is willing to bear a public cost.

study.originalLanguages: The bor Jeremiah is lowered into is an ordinary word for a cistern, here empty of water and full only of mud, a detail that turns a common structure into a slow, deniable death sentence. His rescuer's very name, eved melekh, servant of the king, becomes an unintended irony, since it is this foreign servant, not the king's own sworn officials, who actually serves the king's true interest by saving the prophet whose word alone could save the city.

typology.type: The righteous sufferer lowered into a pit and rescued from the mire, Jeremiah 38:6, 13
typology.fulfillment: Echoes Joseph cast into a pit by his own brothers, Genesis 37:24, and anticipates Christ, the greater righteous sufferer brought low by His own people yet raised and vindicated by God, Psalm 40:2.

typology.type: Ebed-melech the Cushite, an outsider who alone acts to save God's prophet, Jeremiah 38:7-13
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the gospel's reach beyond Israel to the nations, grace extended to and through an outsider exactly where the covenant people's own leaders had failed, a pattern fulfilled as the gospel goes out to the Gentiles.

study.christ: Jeremiah, beaten and lowered into the mud for speaking a true and unwelcome word, and rescued only through an outsider's costly intervention, prefigures Christ, the true prophet whom His own people struck down for speaking the truth, yet whom God did not abandon in the depths. Psalm 40:2, He drew me up from the pit of destruction, out of the miry bog, is realized fully in the resurrection, where the greater sufferer than Jeremiah is not merely pulled from mud but raised from death itself.
study.god: God's providence works through the least likely means, a foreign court official rather than any of Judah's sworn officials, to preserve His prophet's life, showing His care is never limited to insiders or to those who hold official power. Yet God's patience with Zedekiah has real limits: the same offer of life through surrender is extended right up to the final secret audience, after which the city's fall becomes the very disaster Jeremiah had always said it would be.

crossReferences.note: Joseph's own brothers throw him into an empty pit before selling him into slavery, the same pattern of the righteous cast down by those closest to them.
He drew me up from the pit of destruction, out of the miry bog, and set my feet upon a rock, language that directly echoes Jeremiah's rescue from the cistern.
Lists prophets who suffered chains, imprisonment, and destitution for their faithfulness, the same suffering Jeremiah endures here for speaking truthfully.
Paul and Silas beaten and imprisoned for proclaiming the truth, yet preserved by God, echoing Jeremiah's own unjust beating and imprisonment.

study.oneStory: A faithful prophet is beaten, jailed, and left to die in the mud for telling Judah's king an unwelcome truth, yet God preserves him through the courage of an outsider while the fearful king who privately believes him forfeits his own last chance at life. This is the Bible's recurring pattern: God's true word suffers at the hands of the very people it was sent to save, yet is never finally destroyed, a pattern that reaches its fullest form in Christ, struck down by His own and raised by God.

sermon.title: Christmas at the Castle, Part 5: Jeremiah 37-39
sermon.why: Carson works through the narrative of Jeremiah's arrest, imprisonment, and rescue from the cistern alongside Zedekiah's fearful, double minded response, tracing exactly the passage's own movement from chapter 37 through the fall of Jerusalem in chapter 39.

quiz.q: Why is Jeremiah first arrested at the Gate of Benjamin?

options.text: The captain of the guard falsely accuses him of deserting to the Chaldeans when he tries to leave the city on personal business.
options.note: Verses 12 to 14 record this false charge and Jeremiah's denial.

options.text: He is caught reading the burned scroll from the previous chapter.
options.note: That scroll incident belongs to chapter 36; his arrest here concerns a false desertion charge.

options.text: He publicly curses King Zedekiah in the marketplace.
options.note: The text records no public cursing; the charge is specifically desertion to the enemy.

options.text: He refuses to pray for the king when asked.
options.note: Jeremiah does pray and respond to Zedekiah's request; his arrest happens later, over the false desertion accusation.

quiz.q: What happens to Jeremiah in the cistern of Malchiah?

options.text: He is lowered by ropes into mud with no water, left to starve, then rescued by Ebed-melech the Cushite.
options.note: Verses 6 to 13 record both the intended slow death and the rescue.

options.text: He drowns in the water at the bottom of the cistern.
options.note: The text says the cistern had no water, only mud; he sinks into mud, not water.

options.text: He escapes on his own before anyone notices.
options.note: He is rescued by Ebed-melech's intervention and the king's men, not by his own escape.

options.text: King Zedekiah personally lowers him into the cistern.
options.note: It is the officials who do this, and Zedekiah says he cannot stop them, not that he did it himself.

quiz.q: Who rescues Jeremiah from the cistern, and how?

options.text: Ebed-melech the Cushite, who appeals to the king and then pulls Jeremiah out with ropes padded by old rags.
options.note: Verses 7 to 13 detail this rescue in full, including the padding to protect Jeremiah under his arms.

options.text: The prophet Jeremiah's own family members.
options.note: No family members appear in this rescue; it is Ebed-melech, a foreign court official.

options.text: King Zedekiah's officials who had originally thrown him in.
options.note: Those same officials wanted him dead; it is Ebed-melech who intervenes to save him instead.

options.text: The prophet Baruch, using a rope ladder.
options.note: Baruch does not appear in this rescue scene; Ebed-melech carries out the rescue with ropes and rags.

quiz.q: What does Zedekiah do after hearing Jeremiah's word in their final secret meeting?

options.text: He fears his own officials too much to surrender, and tells Jeremiah to hide the true content of their conversation.
options.note: Verses 19 and 24 to 27 show his fear of the officials and his instruction to conceal what was actually said.

options.text: He immediately surrenders to the Chaldeans as instructed.
options.note: He does the opposite; he never surrenders despite hearing the clear offer of life.

options.text: He has Jeremiah executed for treason.
options.note: Zedekiah protects Jeremiah's life throughout, even swearing an oath not to kill him.

options.text: He publicly announces Jeremiah's counsel to all the officials.
options.note: He does the opposite, insisting the conversation be kept secret from the officials.

quiz.q: What is the repeated offer Jeremiah gives Zedekiah throughout these two chapters?

options.text: Surrender to the Chaldeans and both the king and the city will live; refuse and the city will be burned and the king seized.
options.note: This exact offer appears in 38:17 to 18 and is consistent with Jeremiah's message throughout the siege.

options.text: Flee immediately to Egypt for safety.
options.note: Jeremiah never counsels flight to Egypt here; that theme appears later, after Jerusalem's fall.

options.text: Gather an army and fight the Chaldeans directly.
options.note: Jeremiah's consistent counsel is surrender, not armed resistance.

options.text: Offer sacrifices in the temple to appease the LORD's anger.
options.note: The offer is specifically political surrender to Babylon, not a call to ritual sacrifice.

questions: Zedekiah sought God's word repeatedly and privately believed enough of it to protect Jeremiah's life, yet fear of his officials kept him from ever obeying it. Where does fear of what others will think keep you from acting on what you already know is true?

Ebed-melech, an outsider with no political stake in the outcome, was the one person in the palace who acted to save God's prophet. Where might God be calling you to act with costly courage even though it is not officially your responsibility?

Jeremiah told the truth at real personal cost, beaten and nearly left to die for it, yet never stopped repeating the same offer of life. What would it look like for you to keep speaking a hard truth in love even when it is not welcomed?

God preserved Jeremiah's life through mud and imprisonment, not by removing the suffering but by sustaining him through it. Where do you need to trust God's preserving care in a hard season rather than expecting Him to remove the hardship altogether?
Zedekiah asked for the truth secretly but wanted to manage how it was known publicly. Where are you tempted to seek God's counsel privately while avoiding the public cost of actually living it out?

translationNotes.note: To claim his portion likely refers to a family land transaction or inheritance matter in Benjamin, an ordinary personal errand that Irijah's suspicion twisted into a capital charge of desertion.

The cistern of Malchiah having no water but only mud explains why Jeremiah does not simply drown, and also why the officials could claim they had not directly executed him, a slower and more deniable form of killing.

Zedekiah's fear is specifically of Judean deserters already among the Chaldeans, not of the Chaldeans themselves, revealing his fear of local political retribution and mockery rather than the invading army.
